name: DEMO DAY
title: The Stakeholders Are Watching
description: Every exec, investor, and enterprise prospect is in the room. Live demo. Three rounds to convince them. Your delivery, your model, your credibility — all on the line.

rounds.setup: The CEO opens by asking about the competitor feature everyone saw on TechCrunch this morning. The room is watching.

actions.text: Pull up the roadmap and walk them through your differentiation strategy.
actions.consequence: Clear, confident, specific. The CEO nods. Two VCs take notes. Patience recovers.

actions.text: Pivot immediately to the live demo. 'Let's just show them.'
actions.consequence: Bold. The room leans forward. The demo hasn't fully loaded. You are buying time with charisma.

actions.text: Acknowledge the competitor and say your approach is 'fundamentally different.'
actions.consequence: Technically true. Dangerously vague. Someone writes 'fundamentally different?' on their notepad.

rounds.setup: The live demo hits a loading spinner. Eight seconds. It feels like eight minutes. The room is very quiet.

actions.text: Narrate what's happening. 'This is live data — watch how it handles the edge case.'
actions.consequence: You turned a disaster into a feature demonstration. One engineer in the back silently applauds.

actions.text: Switch to the pre-recorded backup demo.
actions.consequence: Smooth. Nobody mentions the switch out loud. Everybody noticed. It still looks good.

actions.text: Wait it out. It'll load.
actions.consequence: It loads. After 34 seconds. The CEO has typed something on their phone. You do not know what.

rounds.setup: Q&A begins. First question: 'What does the roadmap look like for the next 6 months?' All eyes on you.

actions.text: Pull out the roadmap document and walk through it milestone by milestone.
actions.consequence: Prepared. Credible. Trust earned. The enterprise lead asks for a copy.

actions.text: Paint an ambitious vision. High level, directional, no specifics.
actions.consequence: Inspiring but unverifiable. Three people are excited. One person is specifically not excited.

actions.text: 'Great question — we'll share a detailed roadmap post-meeting.'
actions.consequence: A classic deferral. Everyone knows what this means. The enterprise lead uncrosses their arms slowly.

victoryConsequence: Demo Day survived. The enterprise lead schedules a follow-up. The CEO says 'nice work' in Slack — in a channel with 200 people. This is the highest praise you will receive this quarter.
defeatConsequence: The room empties faster than expected. The enterprise lead's assistant cancels the follow-up within the hour. Your calendar is now suspiciously clear.

name: MODEL INCIDENT
title: Hallucination in Production
description: The model is returning confidently wrong answers to 3% of live users. Alert fired 8 minutes ago. A journalist already has a tip. Three rounds to contain this before it becomes a crisis.

rounds.setup: Engineering confirms: 3% of live requests returning incorrect outputs with high confidence. What's your first move?

actions.text: Immediate rollback to last stable version. Kill the feature flag now.
actions.consequence: Decisive. Engineering executes in 4 minutes. Affected users drops to 0. Delivery takes the hit.

actions.text: Deploy a guardrail filter hotfix. Monitor closely.
actions.consequence: Engineering moves fast. The filter catches 80% of bad outputs. Risk reduced but not eliminated.

actions.text: Wait for more data. 3% might be noise.
actions.consequence: It is not noise. 3% becomes 4.1% in 20 minutes. Someone has already tweeted.

rounds.setup: A tech journalist tweets: 'Hearing reports of wrong answers with high confidence from [YourProduct].' 847 retweets in 11 minutes.

actions.text: Publish a transparent incident statement with timeline and remediation steps.
actions.consequence: Honest and proactive. The journalist follows up with 'credit where it's due.' Risk drops sharply.

actions.text: Legal drafts a careful response. Hold on public comms until reviewed.
actions.consequence: Measured. The response goes out 2 hours later — accurate, defensible, if not exactly warm.

actions.text: No comment. Let the product speak for itself.
actions.consequence: The silence speaks louder. TechCrunch picks it up by 4 PM. Trust craters.

rounds.setup: The board wants a briefing in 60 minutes. Root cause, impact, remediation, prevention. You have the data. Do you have the narrative?

actions.text: Present a full incident report with root cause analysis and a concrete prevention plan.
actions.consequence: The board is grim but satisfied. 'This is how incidents should be handled.' Risk minimised.

actions.text: Give a tight verbal briefing with the key facts and immediate next steps.
actions.consequence: Clear and direct. They appreciate the brevity. Written report requested by EOD.

actions.text: Send the engineering lead to present. You stay focused on remediation.
actions.consequence: Excellent technical depth, zero PM context. The board asks where you were.

victoryConsequence: Incident closed. The post-mortem is thorough, the prevention plan is credible. A journalist writes a follow-up: 'How [Product] handled its first public AI failure — and what others can learn.'
defeatConsequence: The story metastasised. Enterprise clients paused renewals pending review. Legal is scheduling a full audit. The board has requested an external review committee.

name: ENTERPRISE GATE
title: Procurement & Security Review
description: An enterprise client wants to sign. They have a security team, a legal team, and a 47-question questionnaire. Enterprise Confidence must rise. Three rounds to get through the gate.

rounds.setup: The enterprise security team sends a 47-question vendor security questionnaire. Due in 48 hours. Your security docs are... aspirational.

actions.text: Block a full day with your Security lead to answer every question thoroughly.
actions.consequence: Comprehensive. The security team is visibly impressed. Confidence rises significantly.

actions.text: Answer the 20 most critical questions in depth. Flag the rest for a follow-up call.
actions.consequence: Efficient. They appreciate the prioritisation. A follow-up call is scheduled.

actions.text: Send the standard vendor security template. It covers most of it.
actions.consequence: They've seen templates before. Twelve follow-up questions arrive the next morning.

rounds.setup: Legal flags three data handling concerns: data residency, retention policy, and GDPR sub-processor obligations. Each is a deal-breaker if unresolved.

actions.text: Schedule a formal DPA review with Legal and pause new data ingestion during review.
actions.consequence: Rigorous. Legal is relieved. The enterprise client sees a serious partner. Confidence jumps.

actions.text: Resolve the two easier concerns now. Log the third as a roadmap item.
actions.consequence: Good faith gesture. They see progress. Confidence improves moderately.

actions.text: Explain that your current data handling is 'industry standard.'
actions.consequence: 'Industry standard' is not a legal standard. Their lawyer responds the same day.

rounds.setup: The enterprise CTO asks: 'We need to self-host this model. Our policy doesn't allow external API calls for sensitive data.' This was not in the brief.

actions.text: Present your technical architecture and share a self-host roadmap with timeline.
actions.consequence: 'This is the answer we were hoping for.' The deal moves forward.

actions.text: Offer a dedicated cloud tenant with data isolation as a near-term alternative.
actions.consequence: A practical compromise. They accept it as a bridge. Confidence holds.

actions.text: Escalate to leadership — this is a policy decision above your authority.
actions.consequence: Correct process, poor optics. The CTO expected an answer today. Confidence drops.

victoryConsequence: Deal closed. Security review passed. The enterprise client signs. Their team sends a note: 'Best vendor review process we've been through this year.' Legal gets a copy for the trophy wall.
defeatConsequence: The enterprise client pauses the evaluation. 'We need more time to assess your compliance posture.' This is a polite rejection. They are not coming back this quarter.
